nodes.label: Auto-generating condition values
nodes.snippet:
# Auto-generating condition values

 

When your logic needs every possible combination of condition values, entering them manually is tedious and error-prone. Auto-generate fills the table with all combinations for you: enter the unique values for each condition once, and Kissflow generates the rows.

 
 Auto-generate requires at least **two conditions** in your decision table.
 

## Auto-generate condition values

1. Click **Auto-generate** in the top-right corner of the decision table.

 ![Decision table with the Auto-generate button highlighted in the top-right corner](/migration-assets/auto-generating-condition-values/1.png)
 

2. In the dialog, enter the unique values for each condition you want to combine.
3. Click **Generate**.

![Auto-generate dialog with a condition selected, fields to enter unique values, and an Include button for existing condition values](/migration-assets/auto-generating-condition-values/2.png)

Rows with every combination of the values you entered are added below any existing rows. Combinations that already exist in the table are not duplicated.

## Example

A retail business manages product discounts based on customer type, purchase amount, and membership level.

**Unique condition values**

1. Customer type — Regular, Premium
2. Purchase amount — \> $100, \< $100
3. Membership — None, Gold

**Auto-generated rows**

| Customer type | Purchase amount | Membership |
|---|---|---|
| Regular | > $100 | None |
| Regular | > $100 | Gold |
| Regular

nodes.label: Creating conditions and results
nodes.snippet:
# Creating conditions and results

 

Conditions are the criteria your decision table evaluates; results are the values it returns when a row matches.

## Create a condition

1. Click the **Add** button (+) beside **Conditions**.
2. Enter a name and choose the field type.
3. Optionally, set validations to control what values can be entered in the column.

![Create a condition panel with Settings and Validation tabs, showing fields for name, field type, field ID, help text, and operator](/migration-assets/creating-conditions-and-results/1.png)

After creating the condition, enter its values in the table rows.

![Decision table with Age and Salary conditions and a Loan amount result, with an empty row ready for condition values](/migration-assets/creating-conditions-and-results/2.png)

### Supported field types

Conditions and results support these field types: **Text**, **Number**, **Date**, **Date & time**, **Dropdown**, **Yes/No**, **Currency**, **User**, and **Email**.

### Choosing operators

Each condition evaluates its values with an operator. Choose the operator mode that fits your logic:

- **Single** — evaluate with one operator. Available operators include `=`, `≠`, `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`, and for text fields, **Contains** and **Does not contain**.
- **Range** — evaluate values within a range by choosing a lower-limit operator (`>` or `>=`) and an upper-limit operator (`<` or `<=`). Range mode is available for **Number**, **Currency**, **Date**, and **Date & time** con

nodes.label: Creating a decision table
nodes.snippet:
# Creating a decision table

 

You create decision tables inside an app in the development environment.

## Create a decision table

1. Open your app in the app builder.
2. In the left panel, open the **Logic** tab and click the **Create** button.
3. Enter a name and an optional description, then click **Create**.

![Animation of creating a decision table: clicking the Create button in the app builder's left panel, entering a name and description, and clicking Create](/migration-assets/creating-a-decision-table/4.gif)

A new, empty decision table opens. Add [conditions and results](/docs/build/decision-tables/conditions-and-results) to start defining your logic.

## Rename a decision table

To rename a decision table, click the **More options** button at the top right and select **Rename**. You can change the description the same way.

## Find a decision table

Your decision tables are listed under the **Logic** tab in the app builder's left panel.

![Logic tab in the app builder's left panel listing the app's decision tables](/migration-assets/creating-a-decision-table/3.png)

## Limits

A decision table supports up to **10 conditions**, **5 results**, and **50,000 rows**.

## What's next

- [Creating conditions and results](/docs/build/decision-tables/conditions-and-results)
- [Evaluation settings](/docs/build/decision-tables/evaluation-settings)

nodes.label: Evaluation settings
nodes.snippet:
# Evaluation settings

 

Evaluation settings control how the condition values in each row are combined, and how much of the table is evaluated. To open them, click the **More options** button at the top right and select **Evaluation settings**.

![Animation of opening the Evaluation settings dialog from the More options menu in the top-right corner of a decision table](/migration-assets/evaluation-settings/3.gif)

## Logical operation

Choose how the conditions in a row combine:

- **AND** — all conditions in a row must be true for its results to be returned.
- **OR** — any one condition in a row being true returns its results.

## Evaluation method

Choose how much of the table is evaluated:

- **Evaluate till the first match** — evaluation stops at the first matching row and returns its results.
- **Evaluate all matches** — the entire table is evaluated. Choose how the matched results are returned:
 - **Return all matched values** — returns every matched value (limited to 100 values).
 - **Return the last match** — returns the last matching row's values.
 - **Return the average** — returns the average of the matched values.
 - **Return the maximum value** — returns the largest matched value.
 - **Return the minimum value** — returns the smallest matched value.
 - **Return the total** — returns the sum of the matched values.

 
 Aggregation options (average, maximum, minimum, total) are disabled when any result is non-numeric. Numeric result types are Number and Currency.

nodes.label: Importing data from a CSV
nodes.snippet:
# Importing data from a CSV

 

Admins and members can import condition and result values into a decision table from a CSV file.

 
 Your decision table needs at least **one condition or result** before you can import a CSV file.
 

## Import a CSV

1. Click the **Import CSV** button in the top-right corner and select your `.csv` file. Kissflow checks the file for corruption and delimiter errors — if an issue is found, fix it in the file and upload again.
2. Map the CSV columns to the conditions and results of your decision table, then click **Next**. The first 10 rows of your file are shown as a preview, and columns whose header names match are mapped automatically.

 
 A CSV column can only be mapped to a condition or result with the same data type.
 

 ![Import CSV dialog in the Map Columns step, mapping CSV columns to decision table fields with a dropdown of available fields](/migration-assets/importing-data-from-a-csv/2.png)
 

3. Choose how to handle the imported data:
 - **When importing:** add new rows and delete existing ones, or add new rows and keep existing ones.
 - **In case of errors:** skip the specific rows, or cancel the import.

 ![Import CSV dialog showing options for when importing (add new rows and delete or keep existing ones) and in case of errors (skip specific rows or cancel the import)](/migration-assets/importing-data-from-a-csv/1.png)
 

4. Click **Next**. Your data begins importing.

## Limits

- A decision table holds up to **50,000 rows**.
- Up

nodes.label: Decision tables overview
nodes.snippet:
# Decision tables overview

 

A decision table is a structured representation of business logic for decision-making. Instead of writing nested conditions in code or workflow branches, you organize your inputs (conditions) and outcomes (results) in a table that Kissflow evaluates at runtime.

 

## Why decision tables?

When business logic involves many input combinations, evaluating conditions one by one is error-prone — especially when the rules change over time. Decision tables give you:

- **A single place for complex logic** — conditions and results live in one table instead of being scattered across workflow branches.
- **Readability** — anyone on the team can scan the table and understand the rules.
- **Reuse** — the same table can be evaluated from multiple places, so logic isn't copied and pasted.
- **Business-user ownership** — rules can be changed by the people who own them, without a developer rewriting code.
- **Flexibility for changing data** — adjust values in rows instead of restructuring logic.

## How decision tables work

At runtime, input values are compared against your conditions row by row. Depending on the [evaluation settings](/docs/build/decision-tables/evaluation-settings), the table returns the results of the first matching row, or aggregates results across all matching rows.

![A loan amount approval decision table with a salary range condition, loan amount and interest rate results, and a default condition row](/migration-assets/decision-tables-o

nodes.label: Testing a decision table
nodes.snippet:
# Testing a decision table

 

Testing lets you verify that your decision table evaluates conditions correctly and returns the results you expect — before anything depends on it.

 
 Testing requires at least **one condition and one result** in your decision table.
 

## Test your decision table

1. Click the **Test** button in the top-right corner.
2. In the test panel, enter sample values for each condition.
3. Run the test.

![Test panel with sample Age and Salary condition values entered, a Test button, and the returned Loan amount result shown in a table](/migration-assets/testing-a-decision-table/1.png)

The decision table evaluates your sample values and displays the matching results in a table, following your [evaluation settings](/docs/build/decision-tables/evaluation-settings).

## What's next

- [Using a decision table inside apps](/docs/build/decision-tables/using-in-apps)

# Using a decision table inside apps

 

To evaluate a decision table from inside an app, use the [Kissflow SDK](https://developers.kissflow.com/gettingstarted/). This walkthrough uses a simple example: a bank approves a loan application based on the applicant's income.

## 1. Create the decision table

Create a decision table with an **Income** condition (Range: `>=` lower limit, `<` upper limit) and an **Eligibility** result:

| Income >= | Income \< | Eligibility |
|---|---|---|
| 15000 | — | Approved |
| 10000 | 15000 | Further review |
| 0 | 10000 | Denied |

## 2. Create the form

Create a process and add these fields to its form:

| Field name | Field type |
|---|---|
| Income | Currency |
| Check approval | Button |
| Approved or not? | Text |

![Process form in the form builder with an Income currency field, a Check approval button, and an Approved or not? text field](/migration-assets/using-a-decision-table-inside-apps/1.png)

## 3. Configure the button event

1. Select the **Check approval** button, open the **Event** tab, and click **Add an event**.

 ![Animation of selecting the Check approval button, opening the Event tab, and clicking Add an event to open the code editor](/migration-assets/using-a-decision-table-inside-apps/2.gif)
 

2. In the code editor, read the income value from the form using `getField()` from the [Forms SDK](https://developers.kissflow.com/form/about/):

 ```js
 let IncomeInput = await kf.context.getField("Income_process");
 ```

3. Evalu

nodes.label: Where are decision tables used?
nodes.snippet: This page has moved. See [Where are decision tables used?](/docs/build/decision-tables/where-used).

nodes.label: Where are decision tables used?
nodes.snippet:
# Where are decision tables used?

 

Decision tables fit anywhere a workflow or app must pick an outcome from multiple input factors. A common example is a financial institution automating its loan approval process.

Suppose a bank processes loan applications and needs a systematic way to evaluate eligibility. A decision table can weigh multiple factors — credit score, income, and debt-to-income ratio — and return a decision.

## Conditions

Conditions are the criteria used to evaluate input values. For loan approval, you would define ranges for credit score, income levels, and acceptable debt-to-income ratios.

## Results

Results are the outcomes that correspond to each combination of conditions. The decision table compares the input values against each row and returns the matching result — approved, denied, or requires further review.

| Credit score | Income | Debt-to-income ratio | Eligibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| > 700 | High | Low | Approved |
| 600 – 700 | Medium | Medium | Further review |
| \< 600 | Low | High | Denied |

The table gives a clear, visual representation of every scenario, so the loan process can assess applications automatically and consistently.

## What's next

- [Creating a decision table](/docs/build/decision-tables/creating-a-decision-table)
- [Using a decision table inside apps](/docs/build/decision-tables/using-in-apps)

nodes.label: calendarDays function
nodes.snippet:
The calendarDays function is designed for precision tracking of durations where the start and end dates both represent active periods of time. Unlike standard subtraction, which calculates the gap between dates, calendarDays uses inclusive counting (start date = day 1).

It is highly customizable, allowing you to filter out non-working days or isolate specific remainders like leftover days in a month.

## Function details

**Function**

**Description**

**Syntax**

**Output**

**Output type**

calendarDays

Calculates the inclusive difference between dates, counting both the start and end dates.

calendarDays(date1, date2, "unit", "day\_filter")

5

Number

![](/migration-assets/calendardays-function/1.png)

### Parameters

#### Unit (Default: D)

Determines the mathematical remainder or total you want to see.

- **D**: Returns the total number of days within the range.
 
- **M**: Returns the total number of full months within the range.
 
- **Y**: Returns the total number of full years within the range.
 
- **HR**: Returns the total number of hours between two timestamps.
 
- **MIN**: Returns the total number of minutes between two timestamps.
 
- **SEC**: Returns the total number of seconds between two timestamps.
 
- **MD**: Returns the number of days remaining after full months are subtracted.
 
- **YM**: Returns the number of months remaining after full years are subtracted.
 
- **YD**: Returns the number of days remaining after full years are subtracted.
 

#### **Day\_

You can write expressions and create formulas for your computed fields with the help of our Formula builder.

## What is Formula builder?

The Formula builder is an inbuilt tool in Kissflow that helps you write, edit, and evaluate expressions more effectively.

Enterprise plan users, please explore the [AI add-on](/t/83ylatd/ai-formula-builder) to formula builder.

### Highlights of the feature

- Easy-to-use editor
- Auto-suggest options while using the dot operator "." and calling functions
- Simple drag-and-drop design to include fields and functions on the editor
- Ability to evaluate your expression before saving it
- Handy _Search_ interface to look out for a field or a function

> **Note**:
> 
> Formula fields (computed fields) are read-only. They derive their values dynamically from other field inputs and cannot be edited manually.

## Writing formula using the Formula builder

1. Navigate to the field in your process form for which you would like to write a formula.
2. Toggle the **'Is this a computed field?**' field to 'Yes' to enable it, and the **Formula builder** opens.
3. Enter your formula directly in the Formula editor or enter a prompt in the AI-powered space to generate your formula automatically.   ![](/migration-assets/computing-fields-using-formula-builder/1.png) 
4. Type the logic in the editor.

nodes.label: dateDiff function
nodes.snippet:
The dateDiff function calculates the difference between two specific points in time. It is designed to be flexible, allowing you to measure differences in various units (like days, months, or years) while providing control over what counts as a working day. By default, it ignores weekends, but you can override this behavior or provide a specific array of holiday dates to be excluded from the calculation.

**Syntax** 

```
dateDiff(date1, date2, “unit”, holiday(), [holiday_list])
```

## Parameter details

**Parameter**

**Data type**

**Required/Optional**

date1

Date

Required

date2

Date

Required

unit

String

Required ("day",”year” "month", “hour”, “minute”, or “second”) 

Default is day

holiday

Boolean

Optional 

Default: True

holiday\_list

Array

Optional

- **date1 and date2**: The two timestamps or calendar dates you wish to compare.
 
- **unit**: The interval used for the calculation. Supported strings: Year, Month, Day, Hour, Minute, and Second.
 
- **holiday**: A flag to determine if weekends are counted.
 
 - Use true() to ignore weekends (standard business logic).
 
 - Use false() to include weekends as regular days.
 
- **holiday\_list**: An array of specific dates (for example, \["2026-01-01", "2026-12-25"\]) that you want the function to treat as non-working days, even if they fall on a weekday.
 

## How the algorithm works

1. **Initialize the counter**: The system begins with a total count of 0.
 
2. **Define the calculation window**: The system set

nodes.label: Extracting text from parentheses
nodes.snippet:
Imagine you have a form where users enter their full name and ID in a single field, following a consistent format like **Name (ID)**. For example, a user might enter **Natasha (987334)**.

For automation, reporting, or integration purposes, you often need to isolate just the ID and store it in a separate field. This guide shows you how to do this automatically using a simple formula.

## **The solution: Using a formula to extract text between parentheses**

You can pull a piece of text from within a larger string by finding its start and end points. This is perfect for situations where the data you need is enclosed in parentheses, brackets, or other consistent markers.

To extract the ID, use the following formula on your customer name field:

```
CustomerName.subString(CustomerName.find("(") + 1, CustomerName.find(")") - 1)
```

## **How it works**

This formula might look complex, but it's doing three simple things:

1. CustomerName.find("("): First, it finds the position of the opening parenthesis (.
2. CustomerName.find(")" ): Next, it finds the position of the closing parenthesis ).
3. CustomerName.subString(...): Finally, it extracts the text between those two positions.

The + 1 and -1 is important because they tell the formula to start capturing text _after_ the opening parenthesis and stop before the closing parenthesis.

You are telling the system: Find the text between the parentheses.

## **Step-by-step implementation**

Here's how to apply this in a form builder:

nodes.label: How to display the names of multiple users from a lookup field in a text field
nodes.snippet:
This article outlines how users can automatically populate a text field with the names of users selected in a multi-user lookup field. The goal is to present a readable, comma-separated list of user names based on the record chosen in the lookup.

1. An end-user opens a new record or edits an existing one in the primary form.
2. The user selects an item from the lookup field.
3. The system automatically triggers a formula with the target text field.
4. The formula accesses the selected lookup record's multi-user field (lookupfield.users).
5. The formula counts the number of selected users, retrieves each user's name, and concatenates the user names into a single string separated by a comma.
6. The resulting comma-separated string is displayed in the target text field.

A formula-based approach to achieve the goal:

## **Basic implementation (For one to two users)**

This solution uses nested if statements to handle cases where one or two users are selected in the lookup field.

### **Formula**

```
if(lookupfield.users.length() = 1, concatenate(lookupfield.users.get(1).Name), if(lookupfield.users.length() = 2, concatenate(lookupfield.users.get(1).Name, ",", lookupfield.users.get(2).Name), ""))
```

### **How it works**

- if(lookupfield.users.length() = 1, ...): Checks if there is exactly one user. If true, it returns that user's name.
- if(lookupfield.users.length() = 2, ...): If the first condition is false, it checks if there are two users. If true, it joins the first and

nodes.label: IF function
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✓ Basic ✓ Enterprise

The IF function returns one value if a boolean expression is true and another if it is false. 

The syntax is:

```
IF( , , )
```

For these examples, we’ll assume the number field _Food\_rating_ has a value of six. 

**Datatype**

**Example**

**Output**

Text

IF(Food\_rating > 5, ”Yummy”, ”Yucky”)

Yummy

Number

IF(Food\_rating > 5, 10, 1)

10

Currency

IF(Food\_rating > 5, Currency(100, "USD"), Currency(10, "USD"))

$100

Date

IF(Food\_rating > 5, today(), today().offset(2, "D"))

IF(Food\_rating > 5, Date(1970, 5, 29), Date(2015, 5, 29))

(Today’s date)

5/29/1970

You can also use the IF function when assigning a user to a step or as a condition for whether a step occurs. 

**Use case**

**Example**

**Result**

Assigning a User

IF(Food\_rating > 5, \_created\_by, \_created\_by.manager)

True and false values must either be user or system user fields in the form.

Step assigned to the creator

Step condition

IF(Food\_rating>5,true(),false())

Step happens

## Nested IF functions

You can use nested IF functions that look like this:

`IF(Food_rating>5,”Yummy”,IF(Food_rating>3,”OK”,”Yucky”))`

In this case, 

- If the rating is above 5, _Yummy_.
- If it is 4 or 5, _OK_.
- If it is 3 or less, _Yucky_.

## The importance of data types

Expressions in Kissflow are strongly dependent on the data type you are working with. Functions, methods, fields, and operators only work with the correct data type. If you have trouble with an expression, always start by examining the data type. 

Every field type is associated with a particular data type.

Because expressions are so connected to the data type, we’ve separated the instructions for creating expressions based on the dat

nodes.label: Referencing section fields in a table using the parent field
nodes.snippet:
You may have to reference values from one part of the form in another when building forms. In particular, when working with **Sections** and **Tables** in a form editor, you can use the **Parent** field to pull values from a section into a table.

## Syntax

```
Parent. 
```

> **Note**
> 
> Table-to-table field references are not supported, and fields from a table cannot be referenced within a section.

## Example

Imagine you are building a form to collect employee data. One section of your form, **Employee PII**, contains various fields, such as the employee’s name, date of birth, and contact number.

You also have a **Birthday preference** table in the form where employees can select whether to celebrate their birthdays. For consistency and ease of use, you want to automatically display the employee’s date of birth in this table.

To achieve this, you can use the **Parent** field in the **Formula builder** to refer to the date of birth field from the **Employee PII** section in the **Birthday Preference** table.

```
Parent.Date_of_birth
```

![](/migration-assets/referencing-section-fields-in-a-table-using-the-parent-function/1.gif)

## Advantages of the Parent field in the Formula builder

- Simplifies data entry for users by populating known values.
- Reduces redundancy and potential data entry errors.
 
- Makes forms more dynamic and context-aware.

This data is stored in system fields. The values in system fields cannot be manually set or edited, they are only system-generated. However, they can be displayed or used in expressions and reports. 

Each type of flow has a specific set of system fields associated with it. If you need any help using these fields, please reach out to our support team.

nodes.label: Workflow step expressions
nodes.snippet:
![](/migration-assets/workflow-step-expressions/1.svg)

The lastCompletedStep() function retrieves information from your workflow's most recently completed step.

You can access the approvers' names, the number of approvers, assignment time, approval time, whether the deadline was breached, and so on.

```
lastcompletedstep()
```

For example, to assign a step to the same approvers as the previously completed step:

1. Go to the **Assignees** section and select **More assignment options**.
2. Click **Formula**, enter the expression, and **Save**.
 

```
lastCompletedStep().approvers()
```

The current step is assigned to users who approved the previous step.

![](/migration-assets/workflow-step-expressions/2.gif)

## Available methods

You can use the following methods with the lastCompletedStep() function.

****Method****

****Data type****

****Description****

.approvers()

User

Returns the names of the users who approved the previously completed step.

.assignmentMode()

Text

Returns the mode of assignment:

- Round Robin
- Least Load
- Normal

.approverCountRequired()

Number

Returns the total number of approvals required to complete the previous step.

.deadline()

DateTime

Returns the deadline of the previous step

.assignedAt()

DateTime

Returns the time at which the previous step was started.

.approvedAt()

DateTime

Returns the time at which the previous step was approved.

.Name()

Text

Returns the name of the previous step.

.deadlineBreached()

Boolean

nodes.label: Configuring an external data object
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

External data object refers to the Kissflow artifact that enables users to consume data from external platforms through external data connections. 

## Creating an external data object

To consume data from external platforms, open any app and follow the steps below.

1. Click **Data** (![](/migration-assets/configuring-an-external-data-object/1.png)) on the left navigation bar and select **External data objects**.
 
2. **Create an external data object** window opens. Enter the name and description, and choose the external connection from the drop-down menu. 
 
3. Click **Create**. 
  
 
 ![](/migration-assets/configuring-an-external-data-object/3.gif)

If you don't have any existing connections listed, you can click **\+ New Connection** from the top right corner of the drop-down menu. You’ll be directed to the **Create connection** page. 
 

![](/migration-assets/configuring-an-external-data-object/2.png)

Follow the steps outlined in [Setting up an external connection](/t/x2y3mhr/setting-up-an-external-data-connection) to establish the connection.

### Configuring an external data object

After creating an external data object, you should configure it to consume the data. This process involves three steps.

#### Select table

This section will show a list of all the tables available in your external data connection. You can select the specific table you want to consume from this list as an external data object.

> **Note:** 
> To sele

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Here’s a detailed list of all the edit options available in external data objects. 

### Rename an external data object

To modify the name of an external data object,

1. Go to the left nav and select the **Data** icon.
 
2. Click **External data objects** and locate the object that you intend to rename.
 
3. Click **More options** ![](/migration-assets/editing-an-external-data-object/1.png) next to the respective external data object.
 
4. Enter the new name and confirm the changes in the popup.
 
 ![](/migration-assets/editing-an-external-data-object/5.gif)
 

Alternatively, you can open the External data object directly. Click **More options** next to **Edit**, and proceed to rename it from there.

### Archive an external data object

To archive an external data object, 

1. Go to the left nav and select the **Data** icon.
 
2. Click External data objects and locate the object that you intend to archive.
 
3. Click **More options** next to the respective external data object.
 
4. **Archive** the external data object.
 
 ![](/migration-assets/editing-an-external-data-object/2.gif)

If your external data object is used in other flows, such as lookup and integration, you cannot archive it unless you manually remove the dependencies. 

> **Note:**
> 
> Alternatively, you can open the **External data object** directly. Click **More options** next to **Edit**, and archive it.

#### Unarchive an external data object

nodes.label: Integrating external data object
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

App users who possess **Manage** permission to connect to external data objects can configure actions for specific events triggered within Kissflow. 

For example, you have an HR app designed to handle various employee processes. When a termination request is completed, you want to configure an action to delete the particular employee’s PII from your employee table in the external database. 

To achieve this, you should integrate the termination process and your external platform. Once established, you can configure a trigger within your process so that upon completion of the termination workflow, the corresponding record in your table is automatically deleted.

> **Note:** Integration using external data objects is exclusive to apps at the moment. 

## Accessing integration

Once you've established the external connection and configured the external data object, navigate to the **Logic** icon on the left nav and follow the below steps,

1. Under **Integrations**, click **\+** icon. **Create an integration** window opens.
 
2. Name the integration and click **Create**.
 
 ![](/migration-assets/integrating-external-data-object/1.gif)

## Configuring a trigger

This is a generic how-to for configuring a trigger. To learn more about how each connector works, refer to the [particular connector’s guide](/category/connectors).

1. Create an integration. 
 
2. Choose a connector and select a trigger.
 
3. You can select one of the existing acco

nodes.label: Introduction to external data objects
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

External data object, one of the flow types in Kissflow, helps users to seamlessly access the data stored in external platforms like MySQL and MSSQL, thereby eliminating the need to store it within Kissflow. This approach enhances data privacy and security. 

> **Note:**
> 
> Currently, external data object is limited to Kissflow apps and supports only MySQL and MSSQL (version 2017 and above). However, moving forward, new connectors will be added periodically. 

Traditionally, users had to export data from their external platforms and import it into Kissflow, necessitating data storage within Kissflow systems, which was a major concern for many users. Additionally, manual imports posed synchronization challenges, requiring users to create integrations or write custom code to maintain data accuracy. 

To address these issues, external data objects offer a practical solution. 

You can consume data from external platforms directly in Kissflow, without storing the data anywhere in Kissflow, through an external connection. This process simplifies data integration and ensures real-time, secure access to information.

> **Note:**
> 
> This feature is exclusive to Kissflow’s Enterprise version and will be accessible upon request. To request access, please contact your Kissflow Account managers, or feel free to leave a comment on this post, and the team will reach out to you.

### Quick links

[Setting up an external data connection](/t/x2y3mhr/

nodes.label: Lookup an external data object
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

This real-time synchronization fetches up-to-date data from your external platforms via the configured external object. You have the flexibility to choose the fields you want to display in a specific lookup and can apply filters to showcase the data you need selectively.

Once you've established a [connection to your external platform](/t/x2y3mhr/setting-up-an-external-data-connection) at the account level and [configured](/t/h7y3mhw/configuring-an-external-data-object) your application's corresponding external data object, the data is accessible to various modules in your app's data layer, including Process, Boards, and Forms.

Looking up an external data object works the same way as how you’d look up your existing Process and Boards flows, using the [Lookup field](/t/g9h4azr/lookup-field) from the left side fields panel.

For example, in a leave management process, you want to simplify the request submission by allowing users to select their department from a drop-down list. You can configure a dedicated lookup field to access the Department table from your HR database.

Follow these steps to implement this scenario,

1. Drag and drop the **Lookup** field from the left side fields panel directly onto your form. 
 
2. From the right side properties panel, set up the lookup field by providing basic details like its name, field ID, etc. 
 
3. Under **Choose a flow to look up** menu, select the name of your external data object that contai

nodes.label: Managing external data objects in the production environment
nodes.snippet:
Kissflow plans:

✕ Basic ✓ Enterprise

Upon deploying an app with external data objects used in its lookup fields and integration, you’ll have to connect or reconnect the connection again in production to ensure that it functions seamlessly.

Before initiating connection or reconnection, ensure you have Admin permissions to access your app's Manage external data objects page. If you don’t, contact your Super admin to grant you the necessary permission. 

Super admins can follow the steps listed [here](/t/x2y3mhr/setting-up-an-external-data-connection#sharing-external-connection) to grant Admin access to users. 

> **Note:**
> 
> If you’ve used an external data connection to create multiple external data objects in the same app or different apps and have already deployed the connection in production, skip this step.

## Connecting to an external data connection

For first time deployment, follow the below steps:

### For Admins

1. Go to your account’s home page and open the app. 
 
2. Click **More options** ![](/migration-assets/editing-an-external-data-object/1.png) and choose **Manage external data objects**. On this page, you’ll find a list of external data connections linked with that specific app.
 
 ![](/migration-assets/managing-external-data-objects-in-the-production-environment/1.gif)
 
3. Click **Connect** and set up the connection. You’ll be redirected to the **Connect connections** page. 
 
 For existing connections, you will have the option to **Reconnect**.

nodes.label: Safelisting IPs for external data connections
nodes.snippet:
# Safelisting IPs for external data connections

 

Some external platforms only accept connections from IP addresses you've explicitly trusted. Safelisting Kissflow's outbound IPs in those platforms tells them to treat Kissflow as a trusted source.

Kissflow sends outbound calls — including calls to external platforms from your integrations — through [Google Cloud NAT](https://cloud.google.com/nat/docs/overview), using a fixed set of reserved static IPs.

 
 Not every platform requires safelisting. Check the external platform's own documentation first — if it doesn't require it, you can skip this page.
 

Find your account's domain below and safelist the matching IPs.

## For kissflow.com domain customers

- `35.222.151.216`
